content.text: What the Content Delivery Edge Is

The content delivery edge is the layer of infrastructure that sits between users and an application's origin servers. Its job is to serve content from locations physically close to the user — reducing the distance data travels, cutting round-trip latency, and off-loading requests that the origin would otherwise have to handle.

In Azure, the content delivery edge is implemented by two services: Azure CDN and Azure Front Door. Both use the same global network of PoP (Point of Presence) nodes, but they are optimized for different scenarios. Azure CDN focuses on static asset caching; Azure Front Door adds dynamic traffic acceleration, WAF, and global load balancing. Together, they form Azure's complete content delivery edge offering.

attrs.alt: Azure Content Delivery Edge: cache HIT returns directly from PoP (~5–15ms); cache MISS fetches from origin over Microsoft's private backbone

content.text: The Two Paths Through the Content Delivery Edge
Every request that passes through the content delivery edge takes one of two paths, determined by whether the response is in the edge cache:

the PoP has a fresh cached copy of the response. The PoP returns it directly to the user without contacting the origin. Latency is typically 5–20ms, limited only by the network distance to the PoP. Origin servers receive zero load for these requests.

the PoP does not have the response, or the cached copy has expired. The PoP forwards the request to the origin over Microsoft's private backbone, receives the response, stores it in the cache (if cacheable), and returns it to the user. Origin servers bear the load of cache misses only.

The performance goal is to maximize the cache hit rate — the fraction of requests served from cache. A 90% hit rate means 90% of user requests never reach the origin. A 10% hit rate means the edge is adding a network hop without meaningful caching benefit, which increases latency compared to connecting directly to a well-positioned origin region.

Cache hit rate is the primary performance metric for the content delivery edge. Monitor it in Azure Monitor diagnostics. If the hit rate is below 80% for static asset paths, investigate cache key misconfiguration, short TTLs, or excessive query string variation.
Azure CDN is available in multiple tiers with different feature sets and PoP network coverage:
Static caching, rules engine, HTTP/2, geo-filtering
General static asset delivery at moderate cost
All Standard Verizon + advanced WAF, real-time analytics

Microsoft recommends the Standard Microsoft tier for new deployments because it uses the same PoP network as Azure Front Door and is deeply integrated with Azure Monitor, Policy, and managed identity. The Akamai and Verizon tiers are provided for organizations with existing contracts or specialized needs.
Azure Front Door as the Content Delivery Edge for Dynamic Traffic

Azure Front Door extends the content delivery edge beyond static caching to handle dynamic, authenticated, and WebSocket traffic. It is the recommended edge service when your application has any of: API calls that cannot be cached, user authentication, WebSocket connections, or global load balancing requirements across multiple Azure regions.
Front Door is available in two SKUs:
CDN caching, custom domains, rules engine, health probes, origin failover. Appropriate for most web applications.

adds Azure WAF integration, Private Link origins (origin with no public IP), security analytics, and bot protection. Appropriate for security-sensitive applications or origins that must not be publicly accessible.

# Compare Azure CDN Standard Microsoft vs Azure Front Door Standard
# for a static website with an API backend

# Option A: CDN for static, separate App Service for API (no Front Door)
# - Static assets: CDN endpoint -> Blob Storage origin
# - API: users connect directly to App Service (no edge acceleration)

# Option B: Front Door for both (single edge for all traffic)
# Create a Front Door Standard profile
az afd profile create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myFD \
  --sku Standard_AzureFrontDoor

# Origin group for static assets (Blob Storage)
az afd origin-group create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myFD \
  --origin-group-name staticGroup \
  --probe-request-type HEAD \
  --probe-protocol Https \
  --probe-interval-in-seconds 100 \
  --probe-path "/" \
  --sample-size 4 \
  --successful-samples-required 3

az afd origin create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myFD \
  --origin-group-name staticGroup \
  --origin-name blobOrigin \
  --host-name myaccount.blob.core.windows.net \
  --origin-host-header myaccount.blob.core.windows.net \
  --https-port 443 --priority 1 --weight 1000

# Origin group for API (App Service)
az afd origin-group create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myFD \
  --origin-group-name apiGroup \
  --probe-request-type GET \
  --probe-protocol Https \
  --probe-interval-in-seconds 30 \
  --probe-path "/health" \
  --sample-size 4 \
  --successful-samples-required 3

az afd origin create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myFD \
  --origin-group-name apiGroup \
  --origin-name apiOrigin \
  --host-name myapp.azurewebsites.net \
  --origin-host-header myapp.azurewebsites.net \
  --https-port 443 --priority 1 --weight 1000
Caching Rules and Cache Key Configuration

The cache key is the string used to look up content in the edge cache. By default, the cache key is the full request URL including query string. Misconfigured cache keys are the most common cause of unexpectedly low hit rates.
Controlling the cache key in Azure CDN Standard Microsoft and Azure Front Door:

# Create a Front Door endpoint
az afd endpoint create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myFD \
  --endpoint-name myEndpoint \
  --enabled-state Enabled

# Create a rule set for caching rules
az afd rule-set create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myFD \
  --rule-set-name cacheRules

# Rule 1: Cache static assets for 1 year (origin TTL override)
az afd rule create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myFD \
  --rule-set-name cacheRules \
  --rule-name cacheStaticAssets \
  --order 1 \
  --match-variable UrlFileExtension \
  --operator Equal \
  --match-values "js" "css" "png" "jpg" "svg" "woff2" \
  --action-name CacheExpiration \
  --cache-behavior Override \
  --cache-duration "365.00:00:00"

# Rule 2: Never cache API responses (bypass cache for /api/ paths)
az afd rule create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myFD \
  --rule-set-name cacheRules \
  --rule-name bypassAPICache \
  --order 2 \
  --match-variable UrlPath \
  --operator BeginsWith \
  --match-values "/api/" \
  --action-name CacheExpiration \
  --cache-behavior BypassCache

# Rule 3: Ignore UTM query parameters in cache key
az afd rule create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myFD \
  --rule-set-name cacheRules \
  --rule-name ignoreUTM \
  --order 3 \
  --action-name RouteConfigurationOverride \
  --query-string-caching-behavior IgnoreSpecifiedQueryStrings \
  --query-parameters "utm_source" "utm_medium" "utm_campaign"

# Associate rule set to a route
az afd route create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myFD \
  --endpoint-name myEndpoint \
  --route-name mainRoute \
  --origin-group staticGroup \
  --rule-sets cacheRules \
  --supported-protocols Https \
  --https-redirect Enabled \
  --patterns-to-match "/*"
Custom Domains and TLS at the Edge

The content delivery edge terminates TLS connections at the PoP. This means the TLS handshake — which involves several round trips and certificate validation — happens close to the user, not in a distant Azure region. For users 100ms away from an origin region but 5ms from a PoP, terminating TLS at the edge saves 200–300ms of handshake latency on the first connection.
Azure CDN and Front Door both support custom domains with automated certificate management:

# Add a custom domain to a Front Door endpoint
# (First, add a CNAME: www.example.com -> myEndpoint.z01.azurefd.net in your DNS)
az afd custom-domain create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myFD \
  --custom-domain-name myCustomDomain \
  --host-name www.example.com \
  --minimum-tls-version TLS12 \
  --certificate-type ManagedCertificate

# Check validation status (Front Door issues and validates the certificate)
az afd custom-domain show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myFD \
  --custom-domain-name myCustomDomain \
  --query "{validationState:domainValidationState, validationToken:validationProperties.validationToken, expiresAt:validationProperties.expirationDate}"

# Associate the custom domain with the route
az afd route update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myFD \
  --endpoint-name myEndpoint \
  --route-name mainRoute \
  --custom-domains myCustomDomain

Managed certificates in Azure Front Door and Azure CDN are auto-renewed before expiration. You do not need to track certificate expiry dates or manually renew — the service handles it. If you use a custom certificate (Bring Your Own Certificate from Azure Key Vault), you are responsible for renewal.
Geo-Filtering and Access Control at the Edge

Azure CDN and Front Door support geo-filtering: blocking or allowing requests from specific countries before they reach the origin. Geo-filtering is evaluated at the PoP level, so blocked requests never consume origin bandwidth or compute.

# Block requests from specific countries on a CDN endpoint
az cdn endpoint rule add \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myCDNProfile \
  --endpoint-name myEndpoint \
  --order 1 \
  --rule-name blockCountries \
  --match-variable RemoteAddress \
  --operator GeoMatch \
  --match-values "CN" "RU" \
  --action-name Block

# For Front Door: geo-filtering via WAF custom rule
az network front-door waf-policy rule create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --policy-name myWAFPolicy \
  --name blockGeo \
  --priority 100 \
  --rule-type MatchRule \
  --action Block \
  --match-variable SocketAddr \
  --operator GeoMatch \
  --match-values "CN" "RU"

# Allow-list alternative: block all except specific countries
az network front-door waf-policy rule create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --policy-name myWAFPolicy \
  --name allowOnlyUS \
  --priority 200 \
  --rule-type MatchRule \
  --action Block \
  --match-variable SocketAddr \
  --operator GeoMatch \
  --negate-condition true \
  --match-values "US" "CA"
Origin Shield: Protecting the Origin from Cache Miss Storms

When multiple PoPs across the world all experience a cache miss for the same content simultaneously — for example, right after a cache purge or after a TTL expiration that affects all PoPs at once — they can all simultaneously forward requests to the origin. This is called a cache miss storm or thundering herd problem. A popular piece of content that expires across 250 PoPs at the same time causes 250 requests to hit the origin simultaneously, even if only 1 copy needs to be fetched.

Azure Front Door Premium and Azure CDN address this with Origin Shield: a designated intermediate caching node (a specific PoP region) that acts as a single proxy between all other PoPs and the origin. When any PoP misses, it forwards to the Origin Shield PoP rather than directly to the origin. The Origin Shield checks its own cache; if it misses, it sends one request to the origin. All other PoPs that asked the Shield get the same response when it arrives.
Cache miss on popular content (250 PoPs)
1 request to origin; Shield fans response back to 250 PoPs
All users wait for their PoP→origin round trip
Users behind cached PoPs wait for PoP→Shield→origin round trip (one extra hop)
N/A — avoid miss storms for high-traffic content
High-traffic content; origins with limited capacity

# Enable Origin Shield on a Front Door origin group
az afd origin update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myFD \
  --origin-group-name staticGroup \
  --origin-name blobOrigin \
  --enable-private-link false \
  --origin-host-header myaccount.blob.core.windows.net \
  --priority 1 \
  --weight 1000

# Specify the Origin Shield region (choose a region close to origin)
# (In the portal: Origin Group -> Origin -> Enable Origin Shield -> select region)
# Via ARM/Bicep, set sharedPrivateLinkResource or originShieldEnabled on the origin:
# "sharedPrivateLinkResource": null,
# "originShieldEnabled": true,
# "originShieldLocation": "EastUs"

# Verify origin health and shield status
az afd origin show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myFD \
  --origin-group-name staticGroup \
  --origin-name blobOrigin \
  --query "{enabledState:enabledState, priority:priority, weight:weight}"
HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 (QUIC) at the Content Delivery Edge

Azure CDN Standard Microsoft and Azure Front Door automatically enable HTTP/2 for all HTTPS connections. HTTP/2 provides multiplexing (multiple requests over a single TCP connection) and header compression, which reduces the latency impact of loading many small files (CSS, JS, images) that would each require a separate TCP connection in HTTP/1.1.

Azure Front Door Standard and Premium also support HTTP/3 (QUIC) for clients that negotiate it. HTTP/3 replaces TCP with QUIC (UDP-based), which eliminates the TCP connection setup overhead and is resilient to packet loss without head-of-line blocking. QUIC is especially beneficial for mobile users on high-latency or packet-lossy networks.
one request per connection; browsers open 6 parallel connections per origin. Common on legacy clients or when HTTP/2 negotiation fails.
multiplex up to 100+ requests over one TLS connection; header compression with HPACK. Default on all Front Door and CDN connections from supported clients.
UDP-based transport; 0-RTT connection resumption for repeat visitors; no head-of-line blocking. Supported on Front Door; clients advertise support via Alt-Svc response header.

You do not need to configure HTTP/2 or HTTP/3 on your origin servers. The PoP handles protocol negotiation with clients, and always connects to the origin over HTTPS/HTTP/1.1 or HTTP/2 depending on origin support. Users get the benefit of modern protocols regardless of what version the origin supports.
Video Streaming: CDN Edge Delivery Patterns

Video streaming is one of the highest-volume use cases for the content delivery edge. A live stream or video-on-demand (VOD) asset involves large files (segments of 2–10 seconds each, packaged as HLS .ts or DASH .mp4 segments) that are requested by many viewers simultaneously. CDN caching turns one origin delivery into millions of user deliveries.
The video encoder (Azure Media Services or third-party) outputs HLS/DASH manifest files (.m3u8, .mpd) and media segments to Azure Blob Storage.
Azure CDN or Front Door is placed in front of the Blob Storage origin.
Each viewer's player fetches the manifest from the CDN, then requests segments by URL. Segments are cached at the PoP nearest to each viewer.
Segment TTL is set to match the segment duration (e.g., 6 seconds for a 6-second HLS segment). After expiry, the PoP re-fetches from Blob Storage.
The manifest file (.m3u8) has a short TTL (5–10 seconds for live, longer for VOD) so viewers get updated segment lists quickly for live content.

# Configure CDN for HLS video streaming from Blob Storage

# Set blob-level cache headers for video segments (via Azure CLI)
az storage blob update \
  --account-name mystorageaccount \
  --container-name videos \
  --name "stream/segment001.ts" \
  --content-cache-control "public, max-age=86400"

# For live HLS manifests: short TTL so viewers get updated playlists
az storage blob update \
  --account-name mystorageaccount \
  --container-name videos \
  --name "live/stream.m3u8" \
  --content-cache-control "public, max-age=5"

# Create a CDN rule to enforce caching for .ts segments (override origin TTL)
az cdn endpoint rule add \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myCDNProfile \
  --endpoint-name videoEndpoint \
  --order 1 \
  --rule-name cacheVideoSegments \
  --match-variable UrlFileExtension \
  --operator Equal \
  --match-values "ts" "m4s" "mp4" \
  --action-name CacheExpiration \
  --cache-behavior Override \
  --cache-duration "0.06:00:00"

# Rule for manifests: short TTL
az cdn endpoint rule add \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myCDNProfile \
  --endpoint-name videoEndpoint \
  --order 2 \
  --rule-name cacheManifests \
  --match-variable UrlFileExtension \
  --operator Equal \
  --match-values "m3u8" "mpd" \
  --action-name CacheExpiration \
  --cache-behavior Override \
  --cache-duration "0.00:00:10"
Comparing Azure CDN vs Azure Front Door for the Delivery Edge

Choosing between Azure CDN and Azure Front Door depends on whether your primary need is static asset caching alone, or whether you also need dynamic traffic routing, WAF, and origin health management:
Yes — origin groups with health probes
Yes — origin with no public IP
Web apps with APIs and failover needs

The Azure content delivery edge — implemented by Azure CDN and Azure Front Door — places cached content and connection termination close to users at 250+ global PoP nodes. It reduces origin load, cuts latency, and enables global security enforcement at the edge.
Cache HITs serve responses from the PoP in 5–20ms without touching the origin; cache MISSes forward to the origin over Microsoft's private backbone.
Azure CDN Standard Microsoft is optimized for static asset caching; Azure Front Door adds dynamic traffic acceleration, WAF, and global load balancing.
Cache key configuration determines hit rate: strip tracking query parameters, set long TTLs for immutable assets, and never cache authenticated API responses.
TLS termination at the PoP saves 200–300ms of handshake latency for users far from origin regions; managed certificates eliminate manual renewal.
Geo-filtering, WAF rules, and bot protection execute at the PoP — malicious requests are dropped before reaching the origin.
Monitor cache hit rate, request count, and origin latency in Azure Monitor using FrontDoorAccessLog and CDN diagnostic logs.
